attributes.stem:
A 38-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for ongoing management of his type 2 diabetes mellitus which was diagnosed 2 years ago. His most recent HbA1c was 8.5% (target <7%). He has a medical history of type 2 diabetes and idiopathic pancreatitis. He is a recent ex-smoker and does not consume alcohol. His medication includes metformin 1000 mg twice daily.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His body mass index is 27 kg/m2, which is largely unchanged over the past 2 years. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are unremarkable.

His father recently died from myocardial infarction and he states he is keen on reducing his risk of cardiovascular disease.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate addition to his current drug regimen?
attributes.explanation:
This patient requires intensification of his diabetes management given his suboptimal glycaemic control and significant cardiovascular risk factors, including family history of premature coronary artery disease and previous smoking. The choice of second-line agent should address both glycaemic control and cardiovascular risk reduction whilst being appropriate for his medical history.

Empagliflozin is the most appropriate choice. This sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT-2) inhibitor works by reducing glucose reabsorption in the proximal convoluted tubule, resulting in glucosuria. SGLT-2 inhibitors have demonstrated significant cardiovascular benefits in landmark trials, including reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events, cardiovascular mortality, and hospitalisation for heart failure. These agents are recommended as second-line therapy in patients with type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular risk factors. Under the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme, SGLT-2 inhibitors are subsidised as add-on therapy to metformin when glycaemic targets are not achieved. The modest weight loss associated with SGLT-2 inhibitors (through caloric loss via glucosuria and osmotic diuresis) is an additional benefit. There is no contraindication to SGLT-2 inhibitor use in patients with a history of pancreatitis.

Semaglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion, slows gastric emptying, and reduces appetite. Whilst GLP-1 receptor agonists do provide cardiovascular benefits and promote significant weight loss, they are contraindicated in patients with a history of pancreatitis due to the risk of precipitating recurrent episodes. Given this patient's history of idiopathic pancreatitis, semaglutide would be inappropriate. Additionally, under current PBS regulations, patients must trial SGLT-2 inhibitors before accessing subsidised GLP-1 receptor agonist therapy.

Gliclazide is a sulphonylurea that stimulates insulin release from pancreatic beta cells. Whilst it effectively lowers blood glucose, it does not provide cardiovascular protection and is associated with weight gain and hypoglycaemia risk. Given the availability of agents with proven cardiovascular benefits, gliclazide would not be the optimal choice for this patient with significant cardiovascular risk factors.

Pre-mixed insulin would be premature at this stage. Insulin therapy is typically reserved for patients with very poor glycaemic control (HbA1c typically >10%) or those who have failed multiple oral agents. This patient's HbA1c of 8.5% and use of only one oral agent make insulin initiation inappropriate. Furthermore, insulin therapy is associated with weight gain and does not provide the cardiovascular benefits offered by SGLT-2 inhibitors.

Pioglitazone is a thiazolidinedione that increases insulin sensitivity. This class has largely been superseded by newer agents due to side effects including fluid retention, weight gain, increased fracture risk, and potential bladder cancer concerns. It does not provide the cardiovascular benefits of SGLT-2 inhibitors and would not be an appropriate choice for this patient.
learningPoints: SGLT-2 inhibitors are preferred second-line agents in type 2 diabetes for patients with cardiovascular risk factors, providing both glycaemic control and cardiovascular protection.
GLP-1 receptor agonists are contraindicated in patients with a history of pancreatitis due to the risk of recurrence.
references: RACGP. Management of type 2 diabetes: A handbook for general practice. East Melbourne, Vic: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners; 2020. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/7c05e922-ab1f-4301-aab7-f4f9b638cb66/Management-of-type-2-diabetes-A-handbook-for-general-practice.aspx
Andrianesis V, Glykofridi S, Doupis J. The renal effects of SGLT2 inhibitors and a mini-review of the literature. Ther Adv in Endocrinol Metab. 2016 Dec;7(5-6):212–28.
Lee PC, Ganguly S, Goh S‐. Weight loss associated with sodium‐glucose cotransporter‐2 inhibition: a review of evidence and underlying mechanisms. Obesity reviews. 2018 Dec;19(12):1630–41

Collins L, Costello RA. Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists. [Updated 2022 May 29]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK551568/
